questions.question: Explain the directory structure of a deployed web application in Tomcat, listing all mandatory components.
questions.hint: Remember the standard web application directory structure as per Java EE specifications.
keyPoints: Public resources (HTML, JSP, CSS, JS) in root

questions.question: What is the purpose of the WEB-INF directory in a Tomcat web application?
questions.hint: Think about security and configuration aspects of web applications.

questions.question: Explain the role of the web.xml deployment descriptor in Tomcat servlet development.
questions.hint: web.xml is the heart of web application configuration in Java web applications.

questions.question: How do you deploy a WAR file in Apache Tomcat? Mention all possible methods.
questions.hint: Consider both automated and manual deployment approaches.
keyPoints: Copy WAR file to webapps/ directory (auto-deployment)
Using Ant or Maven build tools

questions.question: What is the Tomcat Manager application and what are its key features?
questions.hint: Think of it as a control panel for managing web applications in Tomcat.
keyPoints: Web-based application for managing deployed web apps
View deployed applications and their status

questions.question: Explain the difference between absolute and relative paths in Tomcat deployment.
questions.hint: Consider how URLs are constructed in a multi-context environment.
keyPoints: Absolute path starts from server root (e.g., /myapp/servlet)
Relative path is relative to current context
Relative paths are more flexible for context changes
Context path is the base for relative paths
Best practice: use request.getContextPath() for dynamic paths

questions.question: Explain the step-by-step process to set up and configure Apache Tomcat for servlet development, including installation, environment setup, and verification.
questions.hint: Think from installation to first servlet deployment.
keyPoints: Download Tomcat from Apache website (choose correct version)
Start Tomcat using startup.bat (Windows) or startup.sh (Linux)
Create test servlet and verify deployment

questions.question: Describe the complete process of deploying a servlet in Tomcat, from creating the source file to accessing it via a browser. Include directory structure and configuration details.
questions.hint: Cover the entire development lifecycle from code to deployment.
keyPoints: Compile servlet using servlet-api.jar in classpath
Place compiled .class file in appropriate package directory
Create web.xml with servlet and servlet-mapping definitions
Package as WAR or deploy exploded directory

questions.question: Compare the development directory structure with the deployed structure in Tomcat, explaining how files are organized in each phase.
questions.hint: Consider the difference between what developers see and what Tomcat sees.
keyPoints: Development: separate source and output directories
Deployment: compressed WAR file or exploded directory
Both require WEB-INF/classes/ for compiled servlets
Both require WEB-INF/lib/ for JAR dependencies
Public resources in root for both
Development may use IDE build folders

questions.question: Explain how Tomcat handles HTTP requests and maps them to servlets, including the role of the Container, web.xml, and URL structure.
questions.hint: Trace the complete request flow from client to servlet.
keyPoints: Tomcat receives HTTP request on configured port
Container parses the URL to extract context path
Container looks up the web application for that context
Container reads web.xml for servlet mappings
Matches URL pattern to servlet class

questions.question: If a web application is deployed at context path "/myapp" with a servlet mapped to "/hello", and Tomcat is running on port 8080, what will be the complete URL to access the servlet? Also, if the servlet is in package com.example and class HelloServlet, what is the file path to the compiled class in the deployed application?
questions.hint: Combine context path, port, and servlet mapping.
keyPoints: Context path is the directory name in webapps/
URL pattern is appended to context path
Package structure translates to directory structure

questions.question: A web application needs to be deployed with the following specifications: Context path: "/shop", Servlet URL pattern: "/products", Tomcat port: 9090. Also, the application uses MySQL JDBC driver (mysql-connector-java.jar) and has an index.html welcome file. Design the complete directory structure and write the web.xml configuration.
questions.hint: Consider all components: directories, files, and configuration.

questions.question: You have a servlet at com.ecommerce.controller.ProductServlet mapped to URL pattern "/product". Design the directory structure for deploying this in Tomcat as a WAR file named "estore.war", and write the minimum required web.xml entries. Also, write the complete URL assuming the default Tomcat port.
questions.hint: Think about how WAR files are structured and deployed.
keyPoints: WAR file gets extracted to webapps/estore/ directory

questions.question: Explain the auto-deployment feature of Tomcat. How does Tomcat automatically deploy applications placed in the webapps directory?
questions.hint: Think about what happens when you drop files in webapps.
keyPoints: Context is reloaded on changes (if reload enabled)
XML descriptors in conf/Catalina/localhost/ can override

questions.question: Discuss the key configuration files in Tomcat server and their purposes. How do they relate to servlet development?
questions.hint: Consider both server-level and application-level configuration.
keyPoints: server.xml: Main configuration, connector ports, hosts
web.xml: Default servlet and JSP configurations
These files control how servlets are loaded and executed
